prompt: Why can't a QA tester verify an AI release-notes feature with a normal assertEquals test the way they would a deterministic function?
choices: Deterministic functions cannot be tested at all
AI features never produce incorrect outputs, so there is nothing to test
It is impossible to measure latency or cost for AI features
The same prompt can produce different wording each run, and there is rarely one single correct output string
explanation: AI output is non-deterministic and there is rarely one exact correct string, so assertEquals on a fixed value fails even when the answer is good. The other options are false: AI does produce errors, latency and cost are measurable, and deterministic functions are testable.

prompt: Which of the following are evaluation APPROACHES (ways to gather judgments), as opposed to metrics? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Human review
LLM-as-judge
Golden / offline datasets
Faithfulness / groundedness
explanation: Golden datasets, human review, and LLM-as-judge are all ways of gathering judgments, so they are approaches. Faithfulness/groundedness is a metric, the thing being measured, not a way to collect the judgment.

prompt: An AI summarizer sometimes adds a feature to the release note that appeared in no merged PR. Which metric most directly captures this problem?
choices: Cost per call
Relevance
Faithfulness / groundedness
Latency
explanation: Inventing a feature that no PR contains is an unsupported claim, which is exactly what faithfulness/groundedness measures. Latency and cost are performance metrics, and relevance asks whether content is on-topic, not whether it is actually supported by the source.

prompt: A PM and QA tester want to ship a prompt change to an AI feature without silently degrading quality. Which practices support safe, evidence-based iteration? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Judge the change by how good it looks on two or three hand-picked demos
Wire the eval suite into CI as a regression gate that runs before each release
Track latency and cost alongside quality so a quality gain isn't hiding a speed or cost regression
Rerun a golden-dataset eval suite after the change and keep it only if scores improve with no regression
explanation: Rerunning a golden suite, gating it in CI, and tracking latency and cost give measured, repeatable evidence across the whole distribution. A couple of hand-picked demos are cherry-picked and can mask regressions on the cases you did not look at.

prompt: Human review is the most trustworthy eval but slow and costly; an LLM judge is fast and cheap but biased. For a gate that must run on *every* pull request, which *trade-off* fits — with what safeguard?
choices: Skip evaluation in CI and rely on production complaints
Full human review on every PR; quality must never be automated
LLM-as-judge for speed, validated against a sample of human labels so you trust its scores
Trust the LLM judge's raw scores without ever checking them against humans
explanation: A per-PR gate needs the speed of an LLM judge, and calibrating it against a sample of human labels keeps its scores trustworthy. Human review on every PR does not scale, skipping evaluation defeats the gate, and trusting raw judge scores ignores its known biases.
